California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 27, ).991
charles Wachob
city Attorney
city of Auburn
1225 Lincoln way
Auburn, cA 95603
Re: Your Request for Informa] Assistance
our FiIe No. I -91-4 64
Dear Mr. wachob:
This is in response to your Ietter requesting advice on
behalf of Auburn City Councilnember Deborah Yue $rith respect to
her duties under the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the
pol-iticat Reform Act (the I'Acttr).1 since your request seeks
general guidance with respect to a variety of decisions, we are
providing informal assistance applicable to the dec.isions
qeneratti.2
OUESTT ON
May Councilmember Yue participate in decisions concerning
improvement of the storm drainage system in the old town area of
Auburn?
CONCLUS I ON
If the decision to improve the drainage in the old town area
will result in new or substantially irnproved services to the
councilmenber's property, the councilmenber nay not participate in
the decision.
1 corr"..,*.nt code sections 81ooo-9101-5. AI1 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18OOO, et seo. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 5 of the california Code of Regulations.
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958044807 o (916)322-5660


Our File No. I-91-464
Page 2
FACTS
The City of Auburn has had problems with flooding in the area
of the city occupied by old town. The oLd town area contains
historic buildings built in the 1850s. In 1986 the area flooded
due to a significant storm, and extensive damage to both private
and public property resulted. The area was declared a disaster
area by the state.
There are two principal drainages that enter the old town
commerc ia 1/ res identia 1 area. The larger is the Lincoln way
Drainage frorn the north and the smaller is the Brewery Lane
Drainage to the south. The confluence of the two drainages lies
beneath old town.
Approxinately 1 square mile, or about 1.6 percent of the total.
city, drains through the area. However, the existing system
cannot handle the runoff from a ninor storn. 
The Lincoln way
Basin can handle 150 cubic-feet per second (cfs) and the Brer.rery
Lane Basin 75 cfs, 
Peak flo!, through the basins from a major
storm could result in 570 cfs runoff into the Lincoln way Basin
and 170 cfs into the Breerery Lane Basin. Approxinately 600
residences are located in the Lincoln Way Basin and 125 in the
Brewery Lane Basin, representing a population of over 1,700.
Councilmernber Yue is rnarried to Herbert
property and reside at 215 Sacranento Street.
owns, with Harry Yue, a parcel at 147 Brewery
tenancy .
Yue. The Yues own
Herbert Yue also
Lane in j oint
Herbert Yue is also the president and z5-percent shareholder
of RHRH, Inc. 
RHRH, Inc. is a small closely held family
corporation. Herbert Yue receives a salary from RHRH, Inc.
RHRH, Inc. owns a parcel on washington street in the old to$rn
area, which is leased by century 2L for real estate offices. 
In
addition, RHRH, Inc. leases property at 289 Washington Street,
which is also in the o1d town area, at which RHRH, Inc. oPerates
the Shanghai Restaurant. The Shanghai Restaurant has sustained
flood damage in the past from major storrns.
The city council has decided that improvenent to the drainage
in the old town area is necessary. councilmember Yue did not
participate in this decision. You have asked whether the
councilmernber nay particiPate in the foJ,}owing decisions:
1. Budgeting Auburn Urban Developnent funds for portions of
the corrective neasures I
2. Consideration of a utility 
user fee to fund the
improve:nents;


Our File No. I-9■
-464
Page 3
3. Contracts i.rith municipal bond underwriters and a bond
counsel in connection with a possible bond issuance;
4. Approval of designating city and Auburn Urban Development
funds for the inprovements,'
5. selection of and contracts with engineers to perform the
proj ect .
ANALYSIS
section 871oo prohibits any public official 
frorn making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official 
has a financial interest. 
Councilmember Yue is a public
of f icial. under the Act. (section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
j.nterest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will. have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official 
or a
member of his or her irnrnediate fanj.Iy or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official. has a direct or indirect interest $rorth
one thousand dollars (S1,0oo) or more.
(c) Any source of i.ncone, other than gifts
and other than Ioans by a conmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($2s01 or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the time vJhen the
decision is made.
t**
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means anv investment or
interest owned bv the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, 
by an agent on behalf of a
public officia], 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,


Our File No. 
■-9■-464
Page 4
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly. or beneficially a 1o-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103 (a) , (b) and (c)
emphasis added.
Presumabl-y, Councilmember Yue's interest in the sacramento
street property at which the Yues reside is greater than S1,oo0'
(Section 87103(b).) Moreover, the councilmember has an indirect
interest in the Brewery Lane property and RHRH, Inc., of a value
equivalent to the interest of her sPouse. (Section 87103(b).)
Presumably l,tr. Yue' s one-half interest in the Brer,rery Lane
property and his 25-percent interest in RHRH, Inc. are both rrorth
more than 51.000. (section 87103 (a) . )
In addition, Mr. Yue has a varj,ety of economic interests by
virtue of his ownership interest in RHRH, Inc. 
Pursuant to
Section 87103, set forth above, these would also be indirect
interests of the councilmernber. For exa:nple, RHRH, Inc. ovrns
property on washington Street. section 82033 defines "interest in
ieal property'r to include a pro-rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual
or i menber of the official's 
inrnediate fanily owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.
Thus, Mr. Yue's 25 percentage interest in RHRH, Inc. eguates to a
25 percent interest in the washington street property. If the
value of this interest in the property is greater than 51,000, the
councilmember will also have an indirect econonic interest in the
Washington Street Property.
Moreover, RHRH, Inc. Ieases property in the old town area on
which RHRH, Inc. operates the Shanghai Restaurant. Since it
appears the Shanghai Restaurant is a subsidiary business of RHRH,
Inc., Mr. Yue has a 25 Percent interest in the restaurantJ and
consequently, councilmelnber Yue also has a 25 percent indirect
interest in the Shanghai Restaurant.
Finally, Century 21 j.s a source of income to RHRH, Inc., and
Mr. Yue owns 25 percent of RHRH, Inc. Thus, 25 percent of the
income fro:n Century 21 is attributable to Mr. Yue. (Section
82030.) If the councilmember's conmunity property interest in
this incone (one-half of I1r. Yue's 25 percent portion of the
income) is $250 or nore during the past 12 months, century 21 L/iI1
also be a source of income to the councilnember.
3 Th" 'rinvestments'r of an individual includes a pro-rata share of
investments of any business entity in which the individual or
irnmediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-
percent interesL or greater. 
(Section 82034,' Regulation LA706;
ReguJ-ation 18236. )


Our Fi■e No. ■-9■-464
Page 5
Thus, to sumnarize, the councilmenber has economic interests
in the followj,ng: (1) the Sacramento Street property; (2) the
Brewery Lane property; (3) RHRH, Inc.; ( ) the Washington Street
property; (5) Century 21; and, (6) the Shanghai Restaurant.
Conseguently, the councilmember is prohibited from making or
participating in decisions which would have a reasonably
foreseeabLe material financial effect on any of these econonic
interests, if the effect j.s distinguishable from the effect on the
public aenerally.
Poreseeabil itv
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governnental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a nere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeabl-e. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
According to your facts, it appears that Councilmember Yue
has interests in a variety of properties that will be benefited by
the proposed i:nprovement of the storn drainage system in that
region. Thus, it is substantially Iikely, if not certain, that
the decisions concerning the inprovement of the flood drainage in
the area wilI have a financial effect on the councilmember,s
property ,
Materia ] itv
The Commission has adopted guidelines to determine whether -
financial effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. The test to deternine materiality
differs depending on whether the property interest of the official
is directly or indirectly affected by the decision.
It appears fron your facts that the councilmenber,s property
will, be indirectly involved in the decision. The councilmember,s
real property is directly involved in a decision if the decision
j.nvolves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sale,
purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion frorn any city,
county, district or other IocaI governmental subdivision, of
councilmember's property; the issuance, denial or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizj.ng a
specific use or uses of her property; the imposition, repeal or
nodification of any taxes or fees assessed or irnposed on her
property; or redevelopment of the councilmember,s property.
(Regulation La7O2.1(a) (3).) 
None of these categories apply in
this case.


Our File No. I-9■-464
Page 6
Regulation L8702.3 sets forth standards for deterrnining
nateriality with respect to governmental decisions which
indireet)-y affect real property. Among other things, the indirect
effect of the decision on an official's 
real property is material
if :
(2) The decision involves construction of, or
improvernents to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, 
and the real
property in which the official 
has an interest wj,lI
receive new or substantially improved services.
Regulation L8702.3 (a) (2) .
You stated that the counci lrnember ' s property will receive new
or substantialty improved services as a result of the drainage
inprovements in the area. Consequently, the decision to inprove
the drainage in the area will have a foreseeable and material,
financial effect on the councilmenber and the councilnenber nay
not participate in the decision.
Since it appears that the decisions will have a foreseeable
and material financial effect on the councilmenber's reaL property
!.re have not analyzed their financial effect on the counciLnenber's
business interests. 
(See oenerallv, Regulation L8702.2.)
'rPubl ic GeneraIIv" ExceDtion
Pubtic officials lrith econonic interests that will 
be
materially affected by a decision nay participate in the decision
if the effect on their interests is not distinguishable fron the
effect on the public generally. 
(Regulation 18703.) The rrpublicrl
consists of the entire jurisdiction of the agency in question.
(fn re owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77.) This is so because all the
residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of the official.
For the 'rpublic generallyrr exception to apply to your facts,
a decision must:
1. Affect a siqnificant seqnent of the population of Auburn,'
and,
2. Affect the segment in substantiallv the same nanner as it
wilI affect the official's 
interests.
You have not provided specific nunbers concerning the
population of Auburn and the nunber of persons in the proPosed
ariinage improvement area who are similarlv situated as
councilmember Yue. You stated that 1,700 persons lived in the


Our File No. I-91-464
Page 7
basin area. If r.re assuned that all those persons would be
affected in the sane manner as the councilmenber by the decision,
since the segaent in the basin area constitutes 16 percent of the
L0,515 population of the city of Auburn, the "PubIic generally'l
exception would aPPIY.
Hovrever, it does not appear that the number of persons in the
area which will benefit frorn the flood drainage improvements are
affected in a manner substantiallv sirnilar to councilmember Yue.
Councilrnember Yue has three parcels of real property, a source of
income and a business interest that could be greatly benefited by
the irnprovement to the draj.nage system.4 Th" number of persons
with such extensive econornic interests in the area rvould appear to
be relatively snalI. 
Taking this factor into consideration, it
does not app6ar that a significant segnent of the population of
Auburn will be affected in substantially the same nanner as the
councj,Imember. Thus, the t'public generally" exception would not
apply.
Seqnentation of Decisions
You have asked whether the various decisions relating to the
drainage project may be separated so that the council:nember rnay
participate in future decisions concerning the inprovements' rn
'=oa" 
"ura=, decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separateLy. For exanple, if the resolution of one decision will
eriect the determinatlon of a decision for which the official 
is
disqualified, the councilme:nber must disqualify herself as to
both. Thus, if any of the financing decisions set forth in your
Ietter could affect the proiect as a whoIe, the councilmernber may
not particiPate.
Holrever, we have advised in past letters that under some
circumstances, decisions implementing projects may be treated
separately. For example, in the Moe Advice Letter (No' A-89-454)
we'advised that where an official 
has a conflict of interest with
respect to the formation of an assessment district 
due to
r:otlntial effects on a source of income, he nay sti1l participate
in i:nplernenting decisions. we stated3
If the decision is one which is critical 
to
the project and could result in its ternination or
signifi6ant modification, Mr. Abbott must refrain
fr6m participating in it to the extent that the
4 rt i= not crear fron your retter whether alr of the interests
discussed wiII be affected by the improved flood drainage system'
Since alI the property i.s in rather close proxinity to- one
another, we have lssulned that the inproved services wiII be
pi""ia"if to aII of the properties in question. rf this is
incorrect, Please contact us for further advice'


Our File No. I-9■
-464
Page 8
total effect of the project will naterially affect
... lan economic interest]... 
according to the test
set forth above. If the decision is one which
implenents a decision previously made, the effect
of the decision could be measured by assessing the
difference betvreen various inplernentation
alternatives presented.
You stated that future decisions before the city council
include the selection of an engineer and bond underwriter. These
decisions appear to be merel-y implementing decisions which nay be
separable from the project decisions as a r./hole. Thus, while the
project as a whol.e will have a material financial effect on the
councilnenber, this effect nay be disregarded and the
councilmember may participate in the irnplementing decisions if the
decisions wiIl not foreseeably and rnaterially affect the
official's 
econonic interests.
Thus, where Councilrnember Yue has a conflict of interest with
respect to the drainage plan as a whoJ.e, the councilrnember may
participate in Iater implenentation decisions so long as: (1) the
decision wiII not have a material financial effect on her,
d ist inguj.shable from the effect on the public AeneralIy, and (2)
the decision will not in any $ray affect the results of decisions
for which the councilmember was already disqualified. 
(Athan
Advice Letter, No. A-86-094. )
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-59OL.5
S i ncere Iy ,
Scott Ha I Labrin
Aeting ceneral Counsel

CONCLUS I ON If the decision to improve the drainage in the old town area will result in new or substantially irnproved services to the councilmenber's property, the councilmenber nay not participate in the decision. 1 corr"..,*.nt code sections 81ooo-9101-5. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958044807 o (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-91-464 Page 2 FACTS The City of Auburn has had problems with flooding in the area of the city occupied by old town. The oLd town area contains historic buildings built in the 1850s. In 1986 the area flooded due to a significant storm, and extensive damage to both private and public property resulted. The area was declared a disaster area by the state. There are two principal drainages that enter the old town commerc ia 1/ res identia 1 area. The larger is the Lincoln way Drainage frorn the north and the smaller is the Brewery Lane Drainage to the south. The confluence of the two drainages lies beneath old town. Approxinately 1 square mile, or about 1.6 percent of the total. city, drains through the area. However, the existing system cannot handle the runoff from a ninor storn. The Lincoln way Basin can handle 150 cubic-feet per second (cfs) and the Brer.rery Lane Basin 75 cfs, Peak flo!, through the basins from a major storm could result in 570 cfs runoff into the Lincoln way Basin and 170 cfs into the Breerery Lane Basin. Approxinately 600 residences are located in the Lincoln Way Basin and 125 in the Brewery Lane Basin, representing a population of over 1,700.
